title: Observable — How Atoms Share Electrons
logline: Covalent bonds as shared electron pairs — water's bent geometry and polarity from NIST Chemistry WebBook data.

brand.title: Observable
brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder
brand.cta: Follow Observable — one measurement at a time.

science_subject.key_measurement: O–H bond length ~0.958 Å; H–O–H angle ~104.5° (NIST Chemistry WebBook, H₂O)
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific chemistry visualization, water molecule H2O with shared electron pair density between oxygen and two hydrogens, bent geometry ~104.5 degrees, partial negative on oxygen and partial positive on hydrogens, ball-and-stick with transparent electron cloud lobes, neutral dark background, accurate relative angles, NOT TO SCALE on electron cloud exaggeration, 16:9 documentary fidelity

sources.citation: NIST Standard Reference Database 69 — NIST Chemistry WebBook, water (H₂O), InChI=1S/H2O/h1H2
sources.notes: Bond lengths, angles, thermochemical data — U.S. Government work

sources.citation: Pauling, L. The Nature of the Chemical Bond, 3rd ed. (1960) — covalent bond as shared electron pair
sources.notes: Foundational theory text for shared-pair bonding model

sources.citation: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7 — Covalent bonding and molecular geometry
sources.notes: Open educational consensus on VSEPR and polarity

beats.speech_text: Why does water climb up a straw, dissolve salt, and stay liquid across a huge temperature range — when a similar-sized molecule would be a gas?

Atoms with nearly equal hunger for electrons can share them instead of handing them over completely. That shared pair is a covalent bond. In water, oxygen shares with two hydrogens, but not equally — oxygen pulls harder, so the molecule is bent and polar.

We measure bond lengths and angles with spectroscopy and crystallography — then publish them in databases anyone can check. For water, the O–H length and the hundred-four-degree bend are tabulated in the NIST Chemistry WebBook, not guessed from a cartoon.

Here is the shared-pair picture: two hydrogens anchored to oxygen, electron density pulled toward oxygen's side. The cloud is exaggerated for teaching — real electrons are quantum probability, not colored balls on rails.
beats.visualization_prompt: Water molecule covalent bonding diagram, bent H-O-H geometry 104.5 degrees, polar electron density shift toward oxygen, ball-and-stick plus transparent lobes, chemistry education fidelity

beats.speech_text: Covalent bonds build every protein, plastic, and strand of DNA — and water's particular polarity is why life on Earth uses it as solvent. Chemistry is not a bag of rules; it is shared electrons with measurable geometry.

lines: EVIDENCE: NIST Chemistry WebBook bond geometry; Pauling shared-pair model
VIZ: Structural MODEL — electron cloud exaggerated; NOT TO SCALE
